text: The Militant Culture of the Streets: The Revolutionary Left in France, Italy and West Germany in the “Red Decade” This paper analyses the militant culture of the street in different trends of the revolutionary left in France, Italy and West Germany during the so-called “Red Decade” (1967-1977). French, Italian and German left-wing extremists shared comparable strategic approaches, ideologies on violence and rebellious forms of behaviour towards the police. Nevertheless, the militancy practiced at the international level split into different paths, depending on the specific national characteristics of neo-fascist violence, domestic social tensions and the legacy of armed struggle and civil war experienced in each of the respective countries. The Italian left-wing extremism, in particular, was driven by the high frequency of lethal police operations aimed at defending the public order, which led some trends to endorse the use of guns in street protests. Finally, protest policing in each of the respective countries conditioned the extent to which practices of street fighting and armed resistance to police operations were shared by protesters and led to their possible transformation into terrorists.
